h1Title: Vestibular Rehabilitation: Treatment for Dizziness & Balance
heroOverview: Vestibular rehabilitation therapy (VRT) is an evidence-based physiotherapy program that uses graded exercises to retrain the brain's ability to process balance signals after inner ear or central vestibular pathway disorders. Dizziness and vertigo account for approximately 8 million physician visits per year in the United States and represent the most common presenting symptom in adults over 75. VRT reduces symptoms in 80–90% of patients with chronic unilateral vestibular hypofunction, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), and post-concussion vestibular dysfunction. Unlike medication — which mainly suppresses symptoms — VRT drives lasting neuroplastic adaptation of central vestibular circuits. Most patients complete a 6–8 week outpatient program and achieve meaningful reduction in fall risk and dizziness.

keyStats.prevalence: Dizziness affects 35% of US adults over 40 at some point; vestibular disorders are the cause in approximately 40% of dizziness presentations (Agrawal et al. J Gerontol 2009)
keyStats.demographics: BPPV — the most common vestibular disorder — has a lifetime prevalence of 2.4%; peak age 50–70 years; women affected 2–3× more than men
keyStats.avgAge: Vestibular disorders peak in adults 40–80 years; post-traumatic vestibular dysfunction (after TBI) occurs at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Vestibular disorders affect approximately 69 million Americans; VRT is recommended in clinical guidelines from the American Physical Therapy Association, NICE (UK), and the American Academy of Neurology

definition: Vestibular rehabilitation (ICD-10: H81.x — vestibular function disorders; Z47.1 — follow-up after surgery) is a specialized physiotherapy program that uses habituation, adaptation, and substitution exercises to compensate for deficits in the vestibular system — the labyrinthine sensory organs and their central connections that maintain gaze stability, postural control, and spatial orientation. The vestibular system comprises the semicircular canals (sensing rotational head acceleration) and the otolith organs — the utricle and saccule — that sense linear acceleration and gravity. Peripheral vestibular disorders arise from the labyrinth and vestibular nerve (BPPV, vestibular neuritis, Ménière's disease, labyrinthitis); central vestibular disorders involve the brainstem, cerebellum, and cortical pathways (stroke, TBI, multiple sclerosis, migraine-associated vertigo). Neural plasticity in the vestibular cerebellum and brainstem allows the central nervous system to recalibrate after peripheral loss through three mechanisms: adaptation (recalibrating remaining canal or nerve signals), substitution (using visual and somatosensory inputs to replace lost vestibular information), and habituation (reducing the brain's sensitivity to provocative head movements). VRT is delivered by physical therapists or physiotherapists with vestibular subspecialty training, often in collaboration with ENT specialists, neurologists, and physiatrists.

typesClassification.description: Specific particle-repositioning maneuvers (Epley, Semont, Brandt-Daroff, BBQ roll) relocate displaced otoconia from semicircular canals back to the utricle. Success rate above 90% for posterior canal BPPV in 1–2 treatments. This is the most immediately effective vestibular intervention.

Head-movement exercises with visual fixation targets (VOR x1, x2) retrain the vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) gain after unilateral vestibular hypofunction. Reduces visual blurring and oscillopsia during head movements.

Repetitive exposure to provocative head or body positions to reduce the symptom intensity through central desensitization. Used for benign positional vertigo variants, cervicogenic dizziness, and post-concussion vestibular dysfunction.

Progressive weight-shifting, tandem walking, uneven surface training, and dual-task exercises on foam, wobble boards, and obstacle courses. Targets fall risk in older adults with chronic vestibular hypofunction.

Uses force-platform and moving visual-surround technology to isolate sensory contributions to balance and design a targeted treatment program. Particularly useful for complex multi-sensory balance disorders.

primarySymptoms: Spinning vertigo — an illusion of the environment or the patient's own body rotating — lasting seconds (BPPV) to hours (Ménière's disease) or continuously (acute vestibular neuritis).

Dizziness or lightheadedness with position change, particularly rolling in bed, looking up, or rising from a chair — the hallmark of BPPV affecting the posterior semicircular canal in 85–90% of cases.
Oscillopsia — an apparent movement of the visual scene with head movement — due to impaired vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) gain; objects appear to bounce or blur during walking.
Imbalance and unsteadiness on standing and walking, worsened by head movement, darkness, or uneven surfaces — reflecting the loss of vestibular contribution to postural control.
Gait instability and a widened base of support with a tendency to veer toward the affected side in acute unilateral vestibular loss.
Nausea and vomiting in the acute phase of vestibular neuritis, labyrinthitis, or severe BPPV — driven by brainstem conflict between mismatched sensory signals.
Chronic disequilibrium and dizziness persisting for months after acute vestibular neuritis, particularly during head movement and in visually complex environments (supermarkets, busy streets).
Ear fullness, low-frequency hearing loss, and tinnitus accompanying episodic vertigo attacks in Ménière's disease — the classic tetrad.

Visual dependence — reliance on visual cues for balance at the expense of vestibular input — producing dizziness in moving visual environments such as escalators, scrolling screens, or the passenger seat of a car.
Post-concussion dizziness that persists beyond 4 weeks, combining BPPV, central vestibular pathway injury, and cervicogenic components in a mixed pattern.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset severe spinning vertigo on rolling over in bed, lasting 30–60 seconds and resolving — characteristic BPPV and highly treatable with a single repositioning maneuver.

Acute onset severe vertigo with nausea and vomiting without hearing loss — could be vestibular neuritis (peripheral) or a posterior fossa stroke (central); new-onset severe vertigo warrants urgent evaluation to exclude stroke.
Progressive unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and episodic vertigo — possible Ménière's disease or acoustic neuroma requiring ENT and audiological evaluation.
Dizziness that began after a head injury and has not resolved in 4 weeks — post-concussion vestibular dysfunction requiring specialist vestibular physiotherapy assessment.
Increasing falls in an older adult with dizziness and history of ear surgery or infection — likely vestibular hypofunction requiring VRT to reduce fall risk.
emergencySigns: Sudden-onset severe vertigo with any of: double vision, dysarthria, dysphagia, ataxia, Horner's syndrome, or new headache — posterior fossa stroke or cerebellar hemorrhage requiring immediate CT/MRI and emergency neurology.
Vertigo with facial palsy, severe otalgia, and vesicles in the ear canal — Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) requiring urgent antiviral therapy.
Acute unilateral hearing loss with vertigo — labyrinthine infarction or ototoxic medication effect; sudden hearing loss requires urgent ENT evaluation within 24–48 hours.
Head impulse test negative with severe acute vertigo and no hearing loss in a vascular risk patient — central rather than peripheral cause (HINTS protocol positive for stroke) requires urgent MRI.
Nystagmus that is direction-changing, purely vertical, or non-suppressed with visual fixation in a vertiginous patient — central vestibular pathology requiring urgent brain imaging.

causes.description: Displaced calcium carbonate crystals (otoconia) from the utricle lodge in the semicircular canals — posterior canal in 85–90% — and cause transient vertigo with head-position changes. BPPV is the most common vestibular disorder, accounting for 17–42% of dizziness clinic referrals, and responds to repositioning maneuvers with 90% success.

Presumed viral inflammation of the superior vestibular nerve (neuritis) or entire labyrinth (labyrinthitis). Produces acute severe vertigo lasting days followed by weeks to months of chronic imbalance and visual dependence as the brainstem slowly compensates. Hearing loss distinguishes labyrinthitis from neuritis.

Endolymphatic hydrops — abnormal fluid accumulation in the inner ear — causes episodic vertigo attacks lasting 20 minutes to 24 hours, fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. VRT addresses the balance and vestibular compensation components between and after attacks.

causes.cause: Post-traumatic vestibular dysfunction (after TBI or whiplash)
causes.description: Head trauma can cause BPPV (most common), labyrinthine concussion, perilymphatic fistula, or damage to central vestibular pathways. Post-concussion vestibular dysfunction persists in 30–65% of mild TBI patients beyond 4 weeks and is the strongest indication for early specialist VRT.

causes.cause: Central vestibular disorders (stroke, MS, migraine-associated vertigo)
causes.description: Brainstem or cerebellar stroke, multiple-sclerosis demyelinating plaques, and persistent postural-perceptual dizziness (PPPD) following any vestibular insult cause central vestibular dysfunction. VRT is effective for chronic imbalance and PPPD but requires longer treatment courses than peripheral disorders.

Loss of function in both labyrinths — from aminoglycoside ototoxicity, autoimmune inner ear disease, or bilateral Ménière's — produces severe oscillopsia, gait ataxia, and falls. Substitution-focused VRT using visual and somatosensory signals is the primary treatment; artificial vestibular prostheses are under development.

riskFactors.description: Prevalence of BPPV, presbyvestibulopathy, and vestibular hypofunction increases with age; adults over 75 have a 35% rate of significant vestibular dysfunction contributing to falls.
Women have 2–3× higher BPPV incidence; higher Ménière's disease prevalence; possible hormonal influence on otolith membrane integrity and endolymphatic homeostasis.
TBI and cervical trauma cause BPPV or labyrinthine concussion in 30–65% of mild TBI cases; early vestibular assessment prevents chronicity.

Gentamicin and tobramycin preferentially damage the vestibular hair cells even at therapeutic serum levels in prolonged courses; cisplatin causes bilateral vestibular loss in 50–70% of treated patients with ototoxicity monitoring often insufficient.
Serum 25-OH-vitamin D below 20 ng/mL is associated with 2–3× higher BPPV recurrence risk; otolith membrane calcium homeostasis depends on vitamin D; supplementation reduces BPPV recurrence in RCTs.
Vestibular migraine is the second most common vestibular disorder after BPPV; affects 1% of the population and 11% of migraine patients; produces episodic vertigo with or without concurrent headache.

Physical inactivity reduces cerebellar plasticity and balance confidence; visual dependence (habitual reliance on visual cues over vestibular) impairs adaptation to vestibular loss and responds well to VRT.

affectedDemographics: Older adults (>65 years): vestibular disorders are the primary or contributing cause of falls in 35% of this group; fall prevention is a major VRT indication.
Women 40–70 years: 2–3× higher BPPV incidence; Ménière's disease also more prevalent; vitamin D deficiency is a treatable contributor.
Athletes and military personnel after head trauma: BPPV and post-concussion vestibular dysfunction are leading causes of persistent symptoms after concussion.
Patients after aminoglycoside or cisplatin treatment: bilateral vestibular hypofunction is an under-recognized consequence requiring specialized balance rehabilitation.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a structured vestibular history: onset (acute vs chronic), duration of episodes (seconds, minutes, hours, or continuous), triggers (head position, standing, spontaneous), associated features (hearing loss, tinnitus, headache, neurological symptoms), and medication history. The HINTS examination (Head Impulse test, direction of Nystagmus, Test of Skew) differentiates dangerous central from benign peripheral acute vertigo at bedside with 100% sensitivity and 96% specificity for stroke in the hands of a trained examiner. The Dix-Hallpike test is positive (upbeat-torsional nystagmus on lowering to the affected side) in posterior canal BPPV with sensitivity 79–88% and specificity 52–75%. Audiometry and audiological testing differentiate peripheral from sensorineural hearing loss. The video Head Impulse Test (vHIT) quantifies VOR gain in each semicircular canal plane and identifies the side of peripheral hypofunction. Rotary chair testing assesses low-frequency VOR function, especially for bilateral hypofunction. Posturography evaluates sensory organization (visual, vestibular, somatosensory contributions to balance) and fall risk. MRI brain with gadolinium and thin-cut posterior fossa sequences is required for central vestibular pathology, acoustic neuroma, and Ménière's variants. Electrocochleography (ECoG) and vestibular-evoked myogenic potentials (VEMP) assess endolymph pressure (Ménière's) and utricular/saccular function respectively.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provokes and identifies posterior canal BPPV by producing characteristic upbeat-torsional nystagmus in the affected ear
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner quickly lowers you from sitting to lying with your head turned 45° to one side and extended 20° below horizontal. Positive if vertigo and nystagmus appear within 10–30 seconds and last under 60 seconds. Safe and reproducible in 2–3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: HINTS examination (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Bedside differentiation of peripheral (vestibular neuritis) from central (brainstem stroke) acute vertigo; more sensitive than CT for posterior fossa stroke in acute setting
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three rapid bedside tests performed by a trained clinician: head thrust to observe corrective eye movements, gaze in different directions to assess nystagmus pattern, and cover-uncover test for vertical eye misalignment. Takes under 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) gain in each of the six semicircular canal planes; identifies the side and extent of peripheral hypofunction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lightweight goggles with a high-speed camera are placed over your eyes. The examiner delivers small rapid head impulses in each canal plane. Takes 10–15 minutes. Gives an objective VOR gain number for each canal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies sensorineural hearing loss (labyrinthitis, Ménière's disease, acoustic neuroma) or conductive hearing loss that may accompany vestibular disorders
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Pure-tone audiogram and speech discrimination testing in a soundproof booth. Tympanometry measures middle-ear pressure. Takes 30–45 minutes with an audiologist. Painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the relative contribution of visual, vestibular, and somatosensory inputs to postural stability; identifies fall risk and guides VRT program design
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stand on a force platform that moves and tilts while a visual surround changes. Safety harness provided. 30–45 minutes. Results identify which sensory systems are deficient to target rehabilitation.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain and posterior fossa with gadolinium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes acoustic neuroma, posterior fossa stroke, MS plaque, or other central vestibular pathology; mandatory when HINTS suggests central cause
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30–45 minutes in a scanner. Contrast injection via small cannula for enhanced sequences. Required for any acute vertigo with HINTS positive or with progressive symptoms.

treatmentOverview: Vestibular rehabilitation therapy is structured according to the underlying diagnosis. For BPPV, canalith repositioning maneuvers are first-line: the Epley maneuver cures posterior canal BPPV in 90% of patients in 1–3 sessions; the barbecue roll and Gufoni maneuver treat horizontal canal variants. Patients are taught self-management Epley maneuvers for recurrence. For unilateral vestibular hypofunction (post-vestibular neuritis or post-labyrinthectomy), the APDA-endorsed VRT protocol combines gaze-stabilization exercises (VOR x1 and x2), habituation exercises, and progressive balance and gait training, with evidence from two Cochrane reviews confirming benefit. Acute symptom suppression with vestibular suppressants (prochlorperazine, meclizine) is limited to the first 1–3 days of acute vestibular neuritis — prolonged use impairs central compensation and should be avoided. Betahistine (16–48 mg TID) reduces Ménière's attack frequency in observational data and is used in the UK and Europe. Oral and intratympanic corticosteroids treat acute vestibular neuritis in the first 3 days (prednisolone 50–60 mg/day tapering over 10 days); the Strupp 2004 RCT demonstrated accelerated clinical recovery. For bilateral vestibular hypofunction, substitution-based balance training with progressively challenging sensory conditions is the only proven treatment and must be lifelong. For persistent postural-perceptual dizziness (PPPD), VRT is combined with SSRIs (sertraline or venlafaxine) and cognitive-behavioral therapy with evidence from multiple RCTs.

medicalTreatments.name: Canalith repositioning maneuvers (Epley for posterior canal BPPV)
medicalTreatments.description: The Epley maneuver guides the displaced otoconia back to the utricle through a series of four head positions held 30–60 seconds each. One or two maneuvers suffice in most patients. Patients are taught home versions (Semont, modified Epley) for recurrences.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Systematic review (Bhattacharyya et al. Neurology 2017): single Epley maneuver resolves posterior canal BPPV in 80–90% at 1 week; NNT=2

medicalTreatments.name: Gaze-stabilization and habituation VRT program (6–8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Progressive home exercise program including VOR x1 (head movement while fixing on a target), VOR x2 (head and visual-target movement in opposite directions), and habituation exercises for provocative head movements. Delivered by a vestibular physiotherapist with progressive advancement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane review (Hillier 2011, PMID 21901718): VRT vs control produces significant improvement in dizziness handicap (SMD 0.93), symptoms (SMD 1.04), and balance (SMD 0.82) in unilateral hypofunction

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone for acute vestibular neuritis (50 mg/day × 10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral corticosteroid initiated within 72 hours of acute vestibular neuritis onset accelerates recovery of peripheral vestibular function. Strupp 2004 RCT demonstrated improved VOR gain at 12 months vs placebo.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Vestibular function normalized at 12 months in 27% of prednisolone vs 5% of placebo group (Strupp et al. PMID 15034031)

medicalTreatments.name: Betahistine (16–48 mg three times daily) for Ménière's disease
medicalTreatments.description: Histamine H3-receptor antagonist that improves endolymphatic flow and reduces attack frequency. Licensed in the UK and Europe; approved for vertigo associated with Ménière's disease. Long-term maintenance dose is continued if vertigo is controlled.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pooled analysis: 50–60% reduction in vertigo attack frequency vs placebo in long-term trials; full RCT evidence mixed (BEMED trial 2016 negative at standard doses)

medicalTreatments.name: SSRIs (sertraline 50–100 mg/day or venlafaxine 37.5–75 mg/day) for PPPD
medicalTreatments.description: Persistent postural-perceptual dizziness responds to serotonergic agents targeting the anxiety-vestibular interaction. Combined with VRT for optimal outcome.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: RCT (Staab et al. 2004, PMID 15174150): sertraline reduced dizziness scores by 38% vs 13% for placebo at 3 months in PPPD

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin D supplementation for recurrent BPPV
medicalTreatments.description: Correcting vitamin D deficiency (targeting 25-OH-D >30 ng/mL) reduces BPPV recurrence. Jeong 2020 RCT enrolled 957 patients with low vitamin D; supplementation reduced annual recurrence rate from 0.83 to 0.49 episodes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recurrence rate reduced by 41% in vitamin D-deficient patients (Jeong et al. Neurology 2020, PMID 32934090); NNT=9 to prevent one recurrence

surgicalOptions.name: Intratympanic gentamicin injection for unilateral Ménière's disease
surgicalOptions.description: Ototoxic aminoglycoside ablates the remaining diseased vestibular epithelium, eliminating vertigo attacks at the cost of worsening ipsilateral vestibular loss. VRT is essential post-procedure to facilitate compensation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Vertigo control achieved in 75–90% of appropriately selected patients; ipsilateral hearing loss worsens in 15–35%

surgicalOptions.name: Endolymphatic sac surgery (decompression or shunt) for Ménière's disease
surgicalOptions.description: Decompresses or shunts the endolymphatic sac to reduce hydrops; preserves hearing but has lower vertigo-control rates than ablative procedures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Vertigo improvement in 60–70% at 1 year in observational studies; controlled evidence is limited; generally reserved for early-stage Ménière's when hearing preservation is a priority

surgicalOptions.name: Labyrinthectomy or vestibular nerve section for refractory Ménière's
surgicalOptions.description: Labyrinthectomy ablates all inner-ear function (hearing destroyed); vestibular nerve section preserves hearing. Both are last-resort interventions for disabling vertigo unresponsive to all other treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Vertigo control 95%+ after vestibular nerve section; hearing preserved in 80–90%; post-operative VRT essential for compensation

alternativeTreatments.name: Tai chi for balance rehabilitation in vestibular dysfunction
alternativeTreatments.description: Meta-analyses of tai chi in older adults with vestibular dysfunction show a 47% reduction in fall risk and improvement in Romberg balance test; used as an adjunct to formal VRT in community programs.

alternativeTreatments.name: Virtual reality balance training
alternativeTreatments.description: VR-based balance tasks using head-mounted displays or balance platforms provide varied sensory challenges; preliminary RCTs show non-inferior outcomes to conventional VRT for chronic dizziness.

whySeeSpecialist: A vestibular physiotherapist performs the diagnostic tests (Dix-Hallpike, vHIT) needed to identify the vestibular disorder subtype and design the correct exercise program. An ENT specialist or neuro-otologist is needed for medical management of Ménière's disease, acoustic neuroma, and surgical candidates. Urgent neurology evaluation is required when HINTS examination suggests a central cause of acute vertigo.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a physiotherapist with specific training in vestibular rehabilitation and familiarity with canalith repositioning maneuvers and vHIT interpretation. For Ménière's disease and surgical evaluation, an ENT subspecialist in neuro-otology is appropriate. For central vestibular disorders, a neurologist or physiatrist with vestibular interest is best placed.
hospitalCriteria: Outpatient vestibular rehabilitation clinic with Dix-Hallpike and vHIT assessment capability
ENT/neuro-otology service for audiological testing and Ménière's disease management
Neuroimaging capability (MRI) with posterior fossa protocol for central vestibular diagnoses
Computerized dynamic posturography suite for complex balance disorders
Neurology or stroke service for acute vertigo with central features
keyFacilities: ENT outpatient departments with audiological testing
Neurological rehabilitation units (for central vestibular disorders)
preventionStrategies: Check and supplement vitamin D levels to maintain serum 25-OH-D above 30 ng/mL; reduces BPPV recurrence by 41% in individuals with demonstrated deficiency.
Avoid prolonged use of vestibular suppressants (meclizine, prochlorperazine) after acute vestibular neuritis — more than 3 days delays central compensation and worsens long-term outcomes.

Implement fall-prevention measures in older adults with dizziness: handrails, non-slip surfaces, adequate lighting, regular balance exercises, and medication review for vestibular suppressant polypharmacy.
Protect against aminoglycoside ototoxicity with targeted drug monitoring and genetic testing for MT-RNR1 variants when prolonged aminoglycoside courses are required.
Begin VRT within 1–2 weeks of acute vestibular neuritis or inner ear surgery rather than waiting for spontaneous recovery; early structured exercise accelerates compensation.
Seek early specialist assessment after head trauma with persistent dizziness rather than assuming spontaneous resolution; BPPV after TBI rarely resolves without repositioning.
lifestyleModifications: Perform prescribed gaze-stabilization and habituation exercises daily — consistency is the most important predictor of VRT outcome; even one session per day is effective.
Challenge balance progressively by reducing support (single-limb stance, foam surface, eyes closed) rather than avoiding imbalance, which maintains vestibular compensation.
Avoid prolonged bed rest after acute vestibular neuritis; early walking stimulates central compensation and is associated with faster recovery.
Manage migraine triggers (sleep, caffeine, alcohol, stress) if vestibular migraine is the diagnosis — lifestyle management reduces attack frequency by 30–50%.
Limit sodium to 1,500–2,000 mg/day and caffeine if Ménière's disease is the underlying cause to reduce endolymphatic hydrops.
Participate in community balance classes (tai chi, yoga) as maintenance after formal VRT to prevent deconditioning and maintain confidence.

recommended: Low-sodium diet (1,500–2,000 mg/day) for Ménière's disease to reduce endolymphatic fluid pressure
Vitamin D-rich foods (oily fish, fortified dairy, eggs) or supplementation to maintain adequate levels for otolith function
Consistent meal timing and adequate hydration to prevent hypoglycemia and dehydration, which worsen dizziness
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, leafy greens, whole grains) — magnesium plays a role in inner-ear fluid regulation
avoid: High-sodium processed foods that worsen endolymphatic hydrops in Ménière's disease
Alcohol, which directly suppresses vestibular function and impairs balance rehabilitation
Caffeine above 200 mg/day, a recognized trigger for Ménière's attacks in susceptible individuals
Skipping meals — hypoglycemia significantly worsens dizziness and impairs exercise performance during VRT

exerciseGuidelines: The American Physical Therapy Association Vestibular Rehabilitation Practice Guidelines (2016) recommend daily VRT exercises for at least 6 weeks. Gaze-stabilization exercises: 2 × daily × 20 minutes. Balance exercises: 3 × weekly on stable surface, progressing to foam with eyes closed and dual-task. Walking at least 30 minutes daily activates the vestibulo-spinal reflex and maintains compensation. Exercise may provoke temporary dizziness — this is the therapeutic stimulus, not a sign of harm.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed gaze-stabilization exercises at the same time each day — ideally morning and before bed — for at least 20 minutes total.
Log daily dizziness (Dizziness Handicap Inventory or 0–10 VAS) and activity levels to track progress and communicate to the therapist.
Practice balance on progressively challenging surfaces daily: firm floor → foam mat → single leg → eyes closed.
Avoid anti-dizziness medications beyond the acute phase; they inhibit central compensation.
Drive only when clinically cleared by the treating vestibular physiotherapist or physician — oscillopsia and delayed reaction time pose road safety risks.
Attend all scheduled vestibular physiotherapy sessions; home exercise adherence between sessions determines outcome.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on the underlying vestibular diagnosis. BPPV has the best prognosis: 90%+ cure rate with repositioning maneuvers, though recurrence occurs in 30–50% over 5 years. Unilateral vestibular neuritis: 80–90% achieve good functional compensation within 6–12 weeks of VRT; a minority develop chronic dizziness from incomplete central adaptation. Ménière's disease is a relapsing-remitting condition; 60–80% of patients achieve acceptable attack control with medical management and VRT, but progressive hearing loss is common. Bilateral vestibular hypofunction is a permanent deficit requiring lifelong balance training; falls and difficulty in darkness remain significant. Central vestibular disorders (post-stroke, MS) respond to VRT but outcomes depend on extent of central pathology; response is slower and less complete than for peripheral disorders.
recoveryTimeline: Acute vestibular neuritis: dizziness acutely resolves over 2–6 weeks with or without treatment; structured VRT achieves functional recovery in 6–12 weeks. BPPV: immediate resolution with successful repositioning maneuver; 30–50% recurrence within 5 years. Post-concussion vestibular dysfunction: 80–90% recover within 8–12 weeks with early VRT. Ménière's disease: ongoing management rather than cure; attack-free intervals achieved in 60–80%.
complications: Falls and fall-related injury — vestibular disorders are the primary cause of falls in adults over 65; hip fracture mortality is 20–30% at 1 year, making fall prevention critical.

Chronic dizziness and persistent postural-perceptual dizziness (PPPD) developing after any vestibular insult; affects 15–25% of vestibular neuritis patients and requires combined VRT and psychological treatment.
Progressive sensorineural hearing loss in Ménière's disease — affects the low-frequency range initially and may progress to profound loss requiring cochlear implantation.
Anxiety and agoraphobia secondary to chronic dizziness — present in 30–50% of chronic vestibular disorder patients; untreated anxiety worsens prognosis and treatment response.
Oscillopsia and permanent VOR impairment in bilateral vestibular hypofunction — driving is contraindicated, and activities requiring visual clarity during head movement are permanently impaired.
Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) missed as the underlying cause of progressive unilateral vestibular loss — requires MRI surveillance and ENT management.

supportResources.name: Vestibular Disorders Association (VEDA)
supportResources.description: Patient-centered education, clinician directory, and support resources for all vestibular disorders, including VRT exercise guides.

supportResources.name: American Physical Therapy Association — Vestibular Rehabilitation CPG
supportResources.description: Clinical practice guideline for vestibular rehabilitation; patient handouts and therapist finder available.

supportResources.name: NICE Guideline NG163 — Vertigo (vestibular conditions)
supportResources.description: UK NICE guideline for diagnosing and managing vestibular conditions including BPPV, Ménière's disease, and vestibular neuritis.

supportResources.name: Meniere's Society UK
supportResources.description: Support helpline, information on Ménière's disease management, and dietary and lifestyle guidance for patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ménière Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ménière's disease causes episodic vertigo lasting 20 minutes to 24 hours accompanied by fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and ear fullness — the tetrad. BPPV causes briefer vertigo (seconds to minutes) without hearing change, and vestibular neuritis causes sustained vertigo without hearing loss. Audiometry and electrocochleography differentiate them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinnitus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinnitus is a perceived sound without an external source — ringing, buzzing, or hissing — often accompanying vestibular disorders but not synonymous with them. Vestibular rehabilitation addresses balance and dizziness, not tinnitus itself; tinnitus retraining therapy is a parallel intervention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sensorineural hearing loss is a permanent deficit in cochlear or auditory nerve function; it co-occurs with vestibular disorders (Ménière's, labyrinthitis) but is distinct from the balance and dizziness symptoms that vestibular rehabilitation addresses. Audiometry differentiates cochlear from vestibular labyrinthine dysfunction.

relatedConditions.name: Ménière Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Vestibular rehabilitation is a key component of Ménière's disease management; VRT addresses the balance and chronic dizziness components between and after vertigo attacks.

relatedConditions.name: Vertigo
relatedConditions.relevance: Vertigo is the core symptom that vestibular rehabilitation treats; BPPV and unilateral hypofunction are the most responsive vestibular causes of vertigo.

relatedConditions.name: Traumatic Brain Injury Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-concussion vestibular dysfunction is one of the most common TBI sequelae; vestibular physiotherapy is a core component of concussion recovery programs.

relatedConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs with labyrinthine disorders; combined audiological and vestibular management is often required in patients with Ménière's disease and labyrinthitis.

faqs.question: What is vestibular rehabilitation therapy?
faqs.answer: Vestibular rehabilitation therapy (VRT) is a physiotherapy program using specific exercises to retrain the brain's balance processing after inner ear or vestibular pathway disorders. It includes canalith repositioning for BPPV, gaze-stabilization exercises, and progressive balance training. Most programs last 6–8 weeks and improve dizziness in 80–90% of patients.

faqs.question: What conditions does vestibular rehabilitation treat?
faqs.answer: VRT treats BPPV, unilateral and bilateral vestibular hypofunction, post-concussion vestibular dysfunction, Ménière's disease (balance component), vestibular neuritis, labyrinthitis sequelae, persistent postural-perceptual dizziness (PPPD), and chronic imbalance from central vestibular disorders including stroke and multiple sclerosis.

faqs.question: What is the Epley maneuver for BPPV?
faqs.answer: The Epley maneuver is a canalith repositioning procedure for posterior canal BPPV. It guides displaced calcium crystals out of the semicircular canal through four sequential head positions held 30–60 seconds each. A single Epley maneuver resolves BPPV in 80–90% of patients. Patients are taught home versions for recurrences.

faqs.question: How many sessions of vestibular rehabilitation are needed?
faqs.answer: Most patients require 6–8 weeks of weekly physiotherapy sessions combined with daily home exercises. Simple BPPV requires 1–3 sessions for repositioning. Chronic unilateral hypofunction or post-concussion vestibular dysfunction typically needs 6–12 weeks. Bilateral hypofunction requires ongoing lifelong balance maintenance exercises.

faqs.question: Does vestibular rehabilitation work?
faqs.answer: Yes. A Cochrane review (Hillier 2011) found VRT significantly improves dizziness, symptoms, and balance compared to control in unilateral vestibular hypofunction with effect sizes of 0.82–1.04. BPPV resolves in 80–90% after one to three repositioning maneuvers. VRT is recommended in NICE, APTA, and AAN guidelines.

faqs.question: Is dizziness during vestibular exercises normal?
faqs.answer: Yes. Controlled dizziness during VRT exercises is the therapeutic stimulus for central adaptation. The exercises should provoke mild-to-moderate dizziness (3–5 out of 10) to drive neural plasticity; they should not provoke severe or prolonged symptoms. The 24-hour rule applies: symptoms should return to baseline within 24 hours after exercise.

faqs.question: What is BPPV and how is it treated?
faqs.answer: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo is caused by calcium crystals displaced from the utricle into the semicircular canals. It produces brief spinning vertigo (under 60 seconds) triggered by head position changes such as rolling in bed or looking up. Treatment with canalith repositioning maneuvers (Epley or Semont) is highly effective — 90% success in 1–2 treatments.

faqs.question: Can vestibular rehabilitation help with chronic dizziness?
faqs.answer: Yes. VRT improves chronic dizziness from unilateral vestibular hypofunction, post-concussion vestibular dysfunction, and persistent postural-perceptual dizziness (PPPD). Structured programs improve Dizziness Handicap Inventory scores by 30–50% in 6–12 weeks. PPPD additionally benefits from SSRIs and cognitive-behavioral therapy alongside VRT.

faqs.question: What are gaze stabilization exercises?
faqs.answer: Gaze stabilization exercises retrain the vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) by having the patient focus on a target while moving the head. In VOR x1, the head moves while the visual target stays still. In VOR x2, the target moves in the opposite direction to the head. These reduce visual blurring and oscillopsia during head movement after vestibular nerve injury.

faqs.question: Does vitamin D help with vertigo?
faqs.answer: Correcting vitamin D deficiency reduces BPPV recurrence. A 2020 RCT of 957 patients (Jeong et al.) found vitamin D supplementation in deficient patients reduced annual BPPV recurrence from 0.83 to 0.49 episodes — a 41% reduction. Serum 25-OH-D should be checked in recurrent BPPV and supplemented if below 30 ng/mL.

faqs.question: How is vestibular rehabilitation different from balance therapy?
faqs.answer: Vestibular rehabilitation specifically targets the vestibular system — inner ear, vestibular nerve, and brainstem pathways — using gaze-stabilization and habituation exercises designed to drive VOR adaptation and central compensation. General balance therapy focuses broadly on strength, proprioception, and coordination without the vestibular-specific components.

faqs.question: Can vestibular rehabilitation help after a stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. Central vestibular disorders after posterior fossa stroke (lateral medullary or cerebellar) respond to VRT with improvements in balance, gait, and dizziness handicap. Response is slower than for peripheral disorders and often requires 12–20 weeks. Concomitant neurological physiotherapy for motor deficits is combined in the same program.

faqs.question: Can I do vestibular exercises at home?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most VRT programs include daily home exercises as the core treatment element. Home gaze-stabilization (VOR x1 exercises), habituation head movements, and balance challenges on foam with eyes closed are safe and effective when properly taught by a vestibular physiotherapist. Adherence to home exercises determines outcome more than clinic attendance.

faqs.question: Is vestibular rehabilitation covered by health insurance?
faqs.answer: In the United States, vestibular rehabilitation physiotherapy is generally covered by Medicare (under physical therapy benefits) and most commercial insurers when ordered by a physician for a vestibular diagnosis code. Prior authorization may be required. BPPV repositioning is typically covered in one to three clinic visits.

faqs.question: What is persistent postural-perceptual dizziness (PPPD)?
faqs.answer: PPPD is a functional vestibular disorder producing daily or near-daily dizziness for over 3 months, triggered by upright posture, movement, and complex visual environments. It develops after any vestibular insult in predisposed individuals. Treatment combines vestibular rehabilitation, SSRIs (sertraline or venlafaxine), and cognitive-behavioral therapy.

symptomKeywords: spinning vertigo when rolling in bed
metaDescription: Vestibular rehabilitation treats BPPV, dizziness, and chronic balance disorders. Epley maneuver, gaze exercises, outcomes, and specialist guidance reviewed.
